headings.text: Fig. 03 · Reef Cross-Section · Healthy vs. Degraded
Fig. 04 · Cumulative Heat Stress vs. Recovery Interval

paragraphs.text: Six biogeographic reef provinces account for 84% of the world's tropical coral habitat. Since 1980, live coral cover has fallen by roughly half — and the trajectory is steepening.

A flat-projection atlas of the planet's reef belts, paired with four-decade bleaching curves drawn from the Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network, AIMS long-term reef monitoring, and NOAA Coral Reef Watch satellite anomalies.

Tropical reef-building corals occupy a narrow thermal envelope — roughly 23–29 °C — confining living reefs to a band straddling the equator. The six annotated systems below represent the largest contiguous reef provinces remaining on Earth.
14% of global reef — the area lost to bleaching, disease and storms between 2009 and 2018 alone, equivalent to all the coral currently living on Australia's reefs.

Each profile renders four-decade transects of live coral cover, sampled by long-term monitoring networks. Bars stack the proportion of substrate classified healthy, bleached, and dead across four reference years.

Mass bleaching frequency since 2010 vs. the 1980–2000 baseline. The biological recovery interval has effectively collapsed: most reefs now bleach again before fast-growing branching corals can re-colonise.
An interactive infographic journal translating NOAA and peer-reviewed ocean science into dense visual stories.

                Schematic · benthic cover composition

Phase-shift schematic: dominant cover transitions from branching Acropora and massive Porites assemblages to fleshy macroalgae and rubble, with corresponding collapse of associated reef-fish biomass.

Recovery window
            10 yr
            Required between mass events

visibleText: Deep Current Vol. 04 · Reef Issue Ocean Health Coral Systems Plastic Tides Temperature Rise Action Atlas Chapter 02 · Living Architectures Coral Systems Six biogeographic reef provinces account for 84% of the world's tropical coral habitat. Since 1980, live coral cover has fallen by roughly half — and the trajectory is steepening. A flat-projection atlas of the planet's reef belts, paired with four-decade bleaching curves drawn from the Global Coral Reef Monitoring Network, AIMS long-term reef monitoring, and NOAA Coral Reef Watch satellite anomalies. Updated 2024.09 Reefs Mapped 112 Data Points 4,820 Source GCRMN · NOAA · AIMS On this page World Reef Atlas Six Reef Profiles Bleaching Trajectory Species Components Thermal Stress Index Field Notes Bleaching Scale Healthy live cover Stressed / pale Bleached Dead / algal turf Depth Profile 0 m 30 m 60 m 90 m 120 m PHOTIC ZONE → MESOPHOTIC Issue Tag Reefs Bleaching SST+ § 01 · Distribution The Reef Belt Robinson · 23.5° N ↔ 23.5° S Tropical reef-building corals occupy a narrow thermal envelope — roughly 23–29 °C — confining living reefs to a band straddling the equator. The six annotated systems below represent the largest contiguous reef provinces remaining on Earth. Fig. 01 · World Coral Reef Distribution, 2024 Reef polygons scaled by area · Mercator-equivalent flat projection N = 112 systems 01 · FLORIDA KEYS 02 · MESOAMERICAN 03 · RED SEA 04 · CORAL TRIANGLE 05 · GREAT BARRIER REEF 06 · NEW CALEDONIA N Reef polygons combine GCRMN's Status of Coral Reefs of the World (2020) with NOAA's Allen Coral Atlas geomorphic baselayer. Annotation indices match the reef-profile cards below. Source · GCRMN 2020 · Allen Coral Atlas v2 · NOAA CRW Headline figure 14% of global reef — the area lost to bleaching, disease and storms between 2009 and 2018 alone, equivalent to all the coral currently living on Australia's reefs. § 02 · Six Profiles Reefs in Decline 1980 → 2024 Each profile renders four-decade transects of live coral cover, sampled by long-term monitoring networks. Bars stack the proportion of substrate classified healthy, bleached, and dead across four reference years. Live Stressed Bleached Dead 01 · Great Barrier Reef AUS · 344,400 km² · 2,900 reefs −54% 1980 2000 2010 2024 60% 40% 20% 0 Live cover · 50% → 23% 02 · Mesoamerican Reef MX·BZ·GT·HN · 1,000 km arc −61% 1980 2000 2010 2024 60% 40% 20% 0 Live cover · 53% → 21% 03 · Coral Triangle ID·PH·MY·PNG·SB·TL · 76,000 km² reef −34% 1980 2000 2010 2024 60% 40% 20% 0 Live cover · 68% → 45% 04 · Red Sea EG·SA·SD·ER · 1,800 km coastline −9% 1980 2000 2010 2024 60% 40% 20% 0 Live cover · 52% → 47% · most thermally tolerant 05 · Florida Keys USA · 360 km reef tract −84% 1980 2000 2010 2024 60% 40% 20% 0 Live cover · 33% → 5% · most degraded province 06 · New Caledonia NC · 1,500 km barrier reef −22% 1980 2000 2010 2024 60% 40% 20% 0 Live cover · 64% → 49% § 03 · Trajectory Bleaching Years, 1985–2024 DHW · Degree-heating weeks Fig. 02 · Global Mass Bleaching Events Reefs experiencing >8 DHW per year 2024 = 4th global event 1998 2010 2014–17 2023–24 1985 1995 2005 2015 2024 0% 20% 40% 60% 80% 100% % REEFS BLEACHED Each bar denotes the share of monitored reef stations recording >8 degree-heating weeks of thermal stress in that year. Coral can recover from short events; the compressed 2014–17 and 2023–24 windows leave no recovery interval. Source · NOAA Coral Reef Watch v3.1 · Eakin et al. 2022 Reefs bleached · 2024 77% 4th confirmed global event DHW threshold 8.0 Beyond which mortality > 50% Recovery window 10 yr Required between mass events 2.4× Mass bleaching frequency since 2010 vs. the 1980–2000 baseline. The biological recovery interval has effectively collapsed: most reefs now bleach again before fast-growing branching corals can re-colonise. § 04 · Components Ecosystem Key 6 functional groups Fig. 03 · Reef Cross-Section · Healthy vs. Degraded Schematic · benthic cover composition 15m depth HEALTHY 52% LIVE COVER · 1985 DEGRADED 14% LIVE COVER · 2024 0m 8m 15m SUBSTRATE — CARBONATE PLATFORM Phase-shift schematic: dominant cover transitions from branching Acropora and massive Porites assemblages to fleshy macroalgae and rubble, with corresponding collapse of associated reef-fish biomass. Component Icons Branching coral Massive coral Anemone Urchin Reef fish Sponge § 05 · Stress Index Thermal Stress by Province Mean DHW · 2014–2024 Fig. 04 · Cumulative Heat Stress vs. Recovery Interval Bubble area scaled by reef extent (km²) DANGER ZONE 04 06 03 01 02 05 LOW HIGH → CUMULATIVE DHW (2014–2024) RECOVERY INTERVAL (yr) ← 14 10 7 4 0 Reefs in the upper-right quadrant face the steepest mortality trajectory: high accumulated heat-stress with insufficient time between events for structural recovery. Florida Keys (05) has already crossed the recovery threshold. They will be smaller, simpler, and dominated by a handful of heat-tolerant species — unless we cut emissions this decade." — Dr. Terry Hughes · ARC Centre of Excellence for Coral Reef Studies Next · Temperature Rise → Conservation Atlas Download dataset (CSV) Deep Current An interactive infographic journal translating NOAA and peer-reviewed ocean science into dense visual stories.
